summary: I am originally from Somalia, where I was born and raised before the civil war forced many of us to leave. Growing up in Somalia shaped my early life, but due to the conflict, my family had to migrate for safety. Somalia will always be my homeland, even though I have lived in several other countries since then.

I grew up in Hargeisa, which is in the northwestern region of Somalia and is the second largest city after Mogadishu. My childhood and early education took place there before the civil war disrupted our lives. Hargeisa holds many memories for me as the place where my family and I lived before migrating.

Mogadishu is the largest city in Somalia, and I often heard about it as the main city in my country. Although I did not live there, it was important in understanding the geography and culture of Somalia. Hargeisa, where I grew up, is the second largest city after Mogadishu.

My mother, sister, and her children migrated to Toronto in late 1990 due to the civil war in Somalia. I also spent many years in Toronto as a newcomer, going to school and adjusting to life there. Toronto was very multicultural, and I relied on its efficient transit system like buses and trains, which was very different from later experiences in Minnesota.

After marrying a U.S. citizen, I moved from Canada to the United States with my husband. We initially stayed briefly in Chicago before settling in Minnesota. Living in the U.S. meant adjusting to a different healthcare system and cultural environment compared to Canada.

Chicago was our first stop in the United States after leaving Canada. We only stayed there briefly before making Minnesota our permanent home. Chicago was part of the transition period as we adjusted to life in the U.S.

Minnesota became our home after leaving Chicago, and I have lived here for over a decade. I had to get used to many things like driving and the colder, snowier weather compared to Toronto. Minnesota is a great place to raise a family, with wonderful parks, schools, and a vibrant community including Somali and non-Somali friends.

While living in Canada, I had a health card from Health Canada which made accessing healthcare straightforward. The universal healthcare system meant I didn’t have to worry about copays or insurance networks like I do in the U.S. Presenting my Health Canada card was a simple process wherever I went in Canada.
